full_response:
A (contravariant) 4-vector at an event is an assignment of four numbers \(V^{a}\) that, under a Lorentz transformation \(x^{a}\rightarrow x'^{a}= \Lambda^{a}{}_{b}\,x^{b}\), transform as  

\[
V'^{a}= \Lambda^{a}{}_{b}\,V^{b}.
\]

Consider instead the operator  

\[
\partial_{a}\equiv\frac{\partial}{\partial x^{a}} .
\]

Change coordinates to \(x'^{a}\).  Using the chain rule

\[
\partial'_{a}\;=\;\frac{\partial}{\partial x'^{a}}
             \;=\;\frac{\partial x^{b}}{\partial x'^{a}}\,
                 \frac{\partial}{\partial x^{b}}
             \;=\;(\Lambda^{-1})^{b}{}_{a}\,\partial_{b}
             \;=\;\Lambda_{a}{}^{b}\,\partial_{b}.
\]

The factor \(\Lambda_{a}{}^{b}=(\Lambda^{-1})^{b}{}_{a}\) is the inverse of the matrix that multiplies the components of a contravariant vector.  Hence \(\partial_{a}\) does not follow the vector law \(V'^{a}= \Lambda^{a}{}_{b}V^{b}\); instead it transforms with the inverse matrix, the rule appropriate to a covector (one-form).

What does obey the vector transformation law is the gradient of a scalar field,
\((\partial_{a}\phi)(x)\); once the operator acts on the scalar, the result is a set of numbers that transform as a covariant vector.  The bare operator \(\partial_{a}\) itself, however, is coordinate-dependent and behaves as a covector, not as a (contravariant) 4-vector.

\boxed{\displaystyle \partial_{a}\ \text{is not a (contravariant) four-vector because under a Lorentz transformation it picks up the \emph{inverse} transformation matrix, i.e.\ it behaves as a covector (one-form), and only its action on a scalar field produces a true tensor object.}}

Confidence: 45%
